project_overview.project_name: Sudan Somoud - Enhancing Community Resilience Project
project_overview.project_development_objective: Improve access to basic services and food security of Select Communities in the Republic of the Sudan
project_overview.beneficiaries: 560,000 IDP and host community individuals in target localities in Kassala, Northern, and River Nile states; 16,000 farmers; 80 agriculture SMEs; members of 16 community consumer cooperatives and food centers

geographic_scope.coverage_type: national scope with initial focus on three states; may scale to additional localities and states
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - partial. Project targets areas with high IDP concentration and relative security/accessibility. Approximately 30% of Sudan's 79 counties directly affected by conflict. Over 5.5 million IDPs as of December 2023, with 3.54 million (64%) in urban centers in target states. Project may shift focus if conflict escalates in target areas.

key_components.component_name: Component 1: Community Led Basic Service Delivery
key_components.description: UNICEF-implemented component providing fast-tracked and medium-term basic services in education, health, WASH, and GBV response. Includes establishment/strengthening of Community Development Committees (CDCs), temporary engagement of frontline workers and volunteers, technical assistance to local authorities, and project management. Services include community learning centers, health/nutrition packages, climate-resilient water supply systems, sanitation facilities, GBV case management, medical care, psychosocial support, and community awareness campaigns.

WFP-implemented component addressing agricultural production and food supply. Includes: (i) climate-smart agricultural and veterinary inputs/services to farming cooperatives (up to US$300/feddan); (ii) agricultural value chain support through matching grants to SMEs (up to US$100,000); (iii) scale-up of community consumer cooperatives and food centers with renewable energy systems and digital enrollment platforms; (iv) project management and implementation costs.

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: UNICEF (Component 1) and World Food Programme (Component 2) as direct grant recipients. Both will engage implementing partners (local/international NGOs, CSOs). First 3 years through grant agreements; assessment at year 3 for remaining 2 years.
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Coordination Platform comprising World Bank (chair), UNICEF, WFP, and implementing partners meeting quarterly or more frequently. Community Development Committees (CDCs) at locality level for community-led planning and prioritization. State and Mahalia-level authorities engaged for coordination and O&M planning.
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: Alternative Procurement Arrangements using UNICEF and WFP rules and procedures. World Bank assessed and approved both agencies' procurement capacity. Procurement activities include consulting services, goods, non-consulting services, and small/medium works. Draft procurement plan to be agreed within 30 days of effectiveness. Emphasis on local procurement where possible; use of UN humanitarian supply routes if conflict escalates.
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: UNICEF and WFP prepare quarterly Interim Unaudited Financial Reports and bi-annual progress reports. World Bank contracts Bank Support and Monitoring Agent (BSMA) for sample-based field verification, aggregation of results, mid-term and completion reports, social cohesion/market dynamics monitoring. M&E software to be used for result sharing and future government handover. Baselines to be set during first year. Mid-term and closing evaluations planned.

identified_risks.risk_description: Federal institutions non-functional; reliance on state and locality-level authorities which are operational but resource-constrained. Federal security apparatus requires coordination.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Implementation by UNICEF and WFP with international/local partners. Community-led decision-making through CDCs. State-level capacity assessments to inform engagement strategy. Engagement strategy to be developed and reflected in PIM.

identified_risks.risk_description: Severe economic deterioration from conflict, hyperinflation, currency depreciation, halt of domestic/international trade, frozen donor aid, rising unemployment. Food prices increased 50% since war started.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project cannot fully mitigate but will maintain basic services and increase local food production to address availability and affordability challenges. Contractual obligations in US Dollar to counteract exchange rate impacts. Monitor daily exchange rates.

identified_risks.risk_description: Multi-sectoral nature and implementation by two agencies adds complexity. Risk of coordination challenges between UNICEF and WFP.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Phased 5-year implementation with initial smaller geographic focus and possible scale-up. Selection of experienced multi-sectoral implementing agencies. Establishment of coordination platform to facilitate collaboration.

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: Complex and dynamic operating environment may challenge UNICEF and WFP capacities despite their adequate capacity and local presence. Weak local institutions in areas of high IDP concentration.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: World Bank hiring international BSMA for complementary technical support and sample-based verification. Early collaboration with capable implementing partners. Capacity building support for CDCs and local authorities. Technical assistance on asset O&M.

identified_risks.risk_description: High risk from conflict context. Risks include: complex local procurement under conflict; inclusion/exclusion errors in Component 1; procurement capture in Component 2; fraud and corruption; fund diversion; restricted supply of goods if conflict escalates.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Regular detailed review of procurement status by UNICEF/WFP with World Bank during missions. Use of UN humanitarian supply routes. UNICEF and WFP established systems and framework agreement with World Bank. BSMA to complement monitoring and verify implementing partner reports. Adequate segregation of duties, procurement committees, vendor due diligence, anti-fraud clauses, electronic tendering.

identified_risks.risk_description: High social risk from FCV context. Specific risks: exclusion of vulnerable groups; security risks (violence, extortion, theft, looting); GBV/SEA/SH; weak community participation; social tensions between IDPs and host communities; increased IDP mobility to project areas; labor/OHS risks; child labor; forced labor in solar supply chains; inadequate monitoring and grievance redress.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Development of Environmental and Social Management Frameworks (ESMFs) by UNICEF and WFP including Labor Management Procedures, Social Risk Management Framework, GBV/SEA/SH Action Plans, Security Management Plans. Rapid local assessments to understand exclusion and social tension risks. Clear uniform approach to CDC formation with minimum quotas for women (40%) and IDPs (30%). Forced Labor Performance Declarations required from solar system suppliers. Established grievance redress mechanisms (CFM) by both agencies with multiple accessible channels. BSMA monitoring of inclusiveness.

identified_risks.risk_description: Sudan's long history of conflict may impact engagement with authorities and communities, impeding implementation and access. Security risks to stakeholders like resistance committees targeted by warring parties. Risk of tensions between stakeholders.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Emphasis on sensitization and consultation with communities during implementation. Ensure IDPs and host communities integral to decision-making. Culturally appropriate consultations to engage women and marginalized communities. Engagement with local authorities to facilitate access procedures. Project will not move forward without consensus and assurances of all stakeholders. Local conflict, political economy, and needs assessments to be conducted.

identified_risks.risk_description: Conflict may escalate in target areas. Risks of increased parallel conflicts, street crime, unexploded ordinances. Impact on project personnel, contractors, beneficiaries, and asset security. Services/infrastructure could be misappropriated, repurposed, or destroyed.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Local conflict, political economy, and needs assessments with management plans. Adjustment of intervention locations as necessary. UNICEF and WFP follow respective policies for safe fund/goods transfer. PIM to outline procedures for ceasing work due to security concerns. World Bank to monitor impact on social cohesion and market dynamics to avoid inadvertent social tension or market distortions.

safeguards_and_social.environmental_category: High environmental and social risk classification
triggered_safeguard_policies: ESS 1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts
ESS 3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management
ESS 7: Indigenous Peoples/Sub-Saharan African Historically Underserved Traditional Local Communities
ESS 10: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure
OP 7.50: Projects on International Waterways (exception approved for small-scale upgrading/modernization of existing schemes)
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - UNICEF and WFP have established Common Feedback Mechanisms (CFM) with multiple inclusive and accessible channels (written, verbal, text, telephone). Three-level informed consent process. Dedicated GRM teams under Accountability to Affected Population Units. Process includes: receipt, registration/logging, referral, case management, feedback provision, closure, reporting, trend analysis, and quality assurance. Escalation matrix and appeals structure to be established. Project-level grievances complement World Bank's Grievance Redress Service and Accountability Mechanism (Inspection Panel and Dispute Resolution Service).
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Conflict has disproportionately impacted women and girls with limited service access, increased protection risks, and food insecurity. Women face safety risks accessing water; limited access to reproductive health services; high GBV incidence. Women-headed households more food insecure (42% vs 31% male-headed). 79% of women not meeting minimum acceptable diet. Project addresses through: (i) establishing/improving GBV response, health, WASH services; (ii) strengthening local GBV service provider capacities; (iii) increasing female employment as service providers, cooperative managers, local labor; (iv) supporting female-owned SMEs; (v) ensuring women integration into CDCs (40% minimum quota); (vi) monitoring women's satisfaction with services and food security. Women and women's groups integral to community consumer cooperatives and food centers.
vulnerable_groups: Internally Displaced Persons (IDPs) - 5.5 million as of December 2023, including 3 million children
Host communities in areas of high IDP concentration
Women and girls (facing GBV, limited service access, food insecurity)
Children (3 million displaced, out-of-school, vulnerable to malnutrition)
Sub-Saharan African Historically Underserved Traditional Local Communities
Poorest quintile (53% practice open defecation, 37% of under-5 stunted)
Persons with disabilities (implied through inclusive service design)
Refugees (1.3 million crossed into neighboring countries)

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly designed for FCV context with conflict sensitivity embedded throughout. Approach focuses on: (i) Belonging and inclusion - shared vision and institutions representing diversity; (ii) Opportunity and resilience - equitable access to services and food across different backgrounds; (iii) Civic empowerment - community participation in planning and decision-making. Project aims to build social cohesion between IDPs and host communities through joint participation in CDCs, shared service benefits, and community-led planning. World Bank will monitor impact on social cohesion and market dynamics to avoid inadvertent creation of social tension or market distortions. Project designed to complement humanitarian aid with medium-term development support. Flexible design allows geographic shift if conflict escalates. Project will not move forward without consensus of all stakeholders.
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Sudan experiencing state collapse with federal institutions non-functional. Open violent conflict between Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF) and Rapid Support Forces (RSF) since April 2023. 30% of Sudan's 79 counties directly affected by conflict. Economic crisis with hyperinflation, currency depreciation, collapsed banking system, ceased government salaries. Long history of coups, political instability, and conflict since 1956. Two civil wars (1955-72, 1983-2005). South Sudan independence in 2011 resulted in loss of 75% oil production. October 2021 military coup dissolved transitional government. Climate change as fragility multiplier - Sudan ranks 179/185 on climate adaptation risk index. Drought affecting 60%+ hydropower-dependent electricity generation. Flooding affects 200,000 people annually. Low socioeconomic development, inadequate infrastructure, high dependency on climate-sensitive sectors.
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Project operates under UN security umbrella to mitigate security risks. UNICEF and WFP selected partly for security considerations and UN security coverage. Project areas currently neutral, not stronghold of either warring party, but relative security is key selection criterion. Risk of conflict escalation in target areas; project may shift locations if needed. Risks to project personnel, contractors, beneficiaries from insecurity, violence, extortion, theft, armed assault, looting, vandalism. Unexploded ordinances pose risks. Project includes Security Risk Assessment and Security Management Plan (WFP). UNICEF and WFP follow respective security policies. PIM to outline procedures for ceasing work due to security concerns. Project design includes provisions to mitigate security risks to project-financed activities.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: yes - extensive. Over 5.5 million internally displaced persons (IDPs) as of December 2023, the largest child displacement crisis in the world (3 million children displaced). 64% of IDPs (3.54 million) are urban populations moving from Khartoum state to neighboring states (Al Jazirah, Red Sea, River Nile, Sennar, White Nile). Over 66% of IDPs reside in host communities in rented accommodations, schools, gathering sites, improvised shelters, and camps. Over 1.3 million individuals have crossed into neighboring countries since conflict onset, putting pressure on host countries. Project targets both IDPs and host communities in areas of high IDP concentration. Project beneficiaries include 560,000 IDP and host community individuals. CDCs include minimum 30% IDP membership. Project aims to support durable solutions for IDPs through provision of basic services and food security. World Bank will conduct assessments on durable solutions for IDPs, urban planning, and post-conflict reconstruction during implementation.
